beats.narration: Every steel part inherits its flaws. Working the metal doesn't erase ingot discontinuities — it reshapes them. Let's trace where they go.

payload.title: INHERITED FLAWS
payload.sub: Working the metal reshapes discontinuities — it never erases them.

beats.narration: It starts in the ingot. Solidification leaves its signature: a shrinkage pipe at the top center, segregation — uneven chemistry — and nonmetallic inclusions.
Roll that ingot into plate, and the pipe and centerline segregation flatten into thin laminations, parallel to the surface. That's why you check plate edges.
Stretch a billet into bar, and a surface crack becomes a long, tight seam. Inclusions string out into stringers. Everything aligns with the working direction.
So here's the rule: discontinuities are inherited. Know the parent shape, and you know where the children live.

payload.sub: Know the parent shape — and you know where the children live.
